reverse-scaling. Furthermore, while we corrected for some omitted
variable bias by using One-Hot Encoding for property categories, the
model still lacks precise geospatial data, meaning the massive price
impact of specific, highly desirable neighborhoods remains uncaptured.
Testing our hypothesis also relied on creating a mathematically
"average" hypothetical listing, which may not perfectly reflect the
nuanced reality of distinct luxury or budget segments in the Canton of
Vaud. Finally, applying the na.omit() function removed listings with
incomplete profiles, which could introduce bias if those missing data
points systematically represented a specific market segment, such as
poorly managed properties.

# Synthesis, Conclusion & Recommendations

## What the Models Agree On

Three findings hold across very different model architectures:

**The Superhost badge does not raise the nightly price.** Four models with completely different mechanics — the linear regression, the logistic regression (GLM Binomial), the spline-based GAM, and the neural network — all return the same answer: after controlling for listing size, room type and other structural features, Superhost status has no measurable positive effect on price. Some models even show a small *negative* effect. This is the most robust single finding in this study.

**Structural features are the real price drivers.** Capacity (`accommodates`), room type, and the number of bathrooms account for most of the explainable variation in price across all models that include them. The GAM shows that an additional bathroom adds roughly 25% to the price; a private room sells for about 30% less than an entire home, all else equal. Reviews and host attributes matter, but their effect is small compared to the physical features a host can describe in a listing.

**Location matters, and matters non-linearly.** Lake Geneva and Lausanne sit at the top of the price surface; rural and alpine areas drop off sharply. The GAM's 2D spatial spline captures this with an effective degree of freedom around 25 — far too curved to approximate with linear coordinates or postcode dummies. Any tool that ignores location will systematically misprice listings outside the canton's average baseline.

## What the Different Lenses Add

The price models and the occupancy model tell complementary halves of the Superhost story:

| Lens | Finding |
|---|---|
| LM, GLM Binomial, GAM, ANN | Superhosts charge **no more** (and slightly less) than regular hosts |
| GLM Poisson | Superhosts get **2.6× more bookings** than regular hosts |

Read together, these results sketch out the Superhost strategy: price competitively, earn through volume. A host who chases the badge by raising prices is doing it backwards. The badge comes from booking volume and consistent review quality — both of which are easier to achieve at a competitive price point.

A second contrast worth highlighting is the Naive ANN versus the Expanded ANN. The naive version, trained only on host variables, predicts a *lower* price for Superhosts (132 vs 160 CHF). Once room and property type are added, the gap collapses to less than a franc. This isn't really a disagreement between models — it's a textbook case of omitted variable bias, and a useful warning against drawing conclusions from raw price comparisons.

## Recommendations for Hosts

For hosts setting or revising their nightly price:

- **Anchor on structure, not status.** What a guest actually pays for is the listing itself: room type, capacity, bathrooms, location. The Superhost badge helps with visibility and booking volume, not with the per-night rate.
- **Use the traffic-light tool as a sanity check, not a target.** The GAM-based recommendation gives a fair-market range based on what comparable listings achieve. A listing flagged "Underpriced" is leaving money on the table; one flagged "Overpriced" risks sitting empty.
- **If you're building toward Superhost status, compete on price.** The 2.6× occupancy boost comes from satisfied guests and a full calendar. Premium pricing makes both harder. Start with competitive pricing, accumulate reviews, then raise prices selectively once the badge is secure.
- **Review scores matter less than you think for price.** Across the pricing models the reward for higher ratings is modest. Reviews matter, but their role is to drive *bookings* — and through them, the badge — rather than to justify a higher nightly rate.

## Recommendations for Airbnb

For the platform itself, the same data points in three directions:

- **A pricing assistant should use the GAM, not a linear formula.** Linear or postcode-based tools will systematically underprice lakeshore listings and overprice rural ones. The 2D spatial spline is the most defensible way to capture local dynamics with the data Airbnb already collects.
- **Coach the "Underpriced" segment.** Roughly 41% of listings in the dataset sit below the GAM's fair-price band. These hosts are the easiest group to help: a recommendation alone could shift their pricing toward market value, lifting both host revenue and platform commission.
- **Add operationalised location features.** Raw coordinates work well for a smoother like the GAM but limit simpler tools. Distance to the nearest lake, city centre, ski lift, and public transport stop would let classifiers like the SVM catch up to the GAM's accuracy — and these features are trivial to compute centrally and serve to every model on the platform.

# Overall Limitations

Five limitations apply to this entire study, not just to individual models:

**Snapshot, no time component.** All models are trained on a single point in time. Seasonal pricing, weekday effects, and event-driven demand spikes (Montreux Jazz Festival, ski season, weekends) are nowhere in the data. The recommendations represent an *average* market price, not a dynamic one. A real production pricing tool would need a time dimension we don't have.

**Listed price as a proxy for willingness to pay.** We treat what hosts charge as a proxy for what guests would pay. This holds on average across a large sample, but it breaks down for listings that sit unbooked at their current price — those listings still shape the model and probably tilt it slightly toward optimistic price levels. A true willingness-to-pay study would need booking data, completed transactions, and ideally A/B tests of competing prices.

**Missing predictors.** Photo quality, amenity counts, listing description sentiment, view from the property, noise levels, distance to public transport — none of this is in the dataset. Some of the unexplained variance (around a third for the GAM) lives in these omitted variables rather than in any model's limitation.

**Reverse causality.** Several relationships in our data probably flow in both directions. More bookings produce more reviews, which make Superhost status easier to maintain, which probably increases visibility, which leads to more bookings. None of our models can untangle which effect causes which. For descriptive purposes that's fine, but the recommendations should be read as "what works on average," not "what works because of X."

**Vaud-specific.** The dataset covers one Swiss canton at one point in time. The price levels, location dynamics (lakeshore premium, alpine resorts), and host behaviours are specific to this market. The methodology transfers; the exact coefficients do not. Applying the same models to Geneva or Bern would require refitting on local data.

# Use of GenAI

Throughout this machine learning project, we leveraged various
Generative AI tools specifically ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude to support
our analytical workflow. A primary use case was troubleshooting complex
issues that extended beyond our existing knowledge base. When
encountering technical bottlenecks, we used these models to break down
and explain the underlying concepts, enabling us to systematically find
the right solutions. Furthermore, we utilized GenAI as a critical review
mechanism for our modeling approach. We consulted the AI to verify
whether we had made the appropriate theoretical assumptions, such as
selecting the correct data distributions for our models. It also served
as a valuable sounding board to double-check our final analytical
interpretations, ensuring our conclusions were statistically sound.
Finally, these tools provided essential linguistic support. We actively
used GenAI to translate our documentation and findings into professional
English, ensuring a high-quality final report.

# Appendix
## Lessons learned
### Pascal
Working on this project was a huge reality check in the best way possible. In our classes, we are used to getting "perfect" datasets that are practically made for the specific algorithms we are learning. But working with our actual `d.airbnb_listings_feat` dataset showed me how much effort actually goes into data cleaning and prepping before you can even start modeling. I personally focused on building the GLM-Poisson and ANN models, and trying to get them to perform well on real, messy data required a lot more critical thinking and trial-and-error than our standard assignments.

Since we split up the workload, I didn't code every single model myself. Instead, my role for the LM, GLM-Binomial, GAM, and SVM models was to check and review the work of my team members. This turned out to be a super valuable learning experience on its own. Reading through their code allowed me to see exactly how other people translate what we learned in class into practice. Seeing their different approaches to problem-solving and troubleshooting bugs really helped expand my own coding mindset.

Finally, figuring out how to collaborate effectively on code was a massive win for us. We used GitHub with RStudio for the first time, and it honestly made a world of difference. We finally found a setup that actually works for coding together as a group, which saved us a lot of headaches and is definitely a workflow I’ll be taking into future projects.
